teaching.title: C Function Structure
teaching.analogy: C functions are the building blocks of every program. No classes, no objects — just pure functions that take inputs and produce outputs. The entire C standard library (printf, scanf, malloc) is just a collection of functions.
teaching.content: C function: returnType functionName(params) { body }. void = no return. Must declare or define before use (prototype rule). C is purely procedural — no methods on objects. All data is passed explicitly.

examples.title: C Function Basics
examples.explanation: Define before use OR provide a prototype.

examples.title: Function Prototypes
examples.explanation: Tell compiler about a function before it's defined.

teaching.funFact: C's header files (.h) are literally collections of function prototypes! When you write #include <stdio.h>, you're including prototypes for printf, scanf, etc. so the compiler knows their signatures before you call them. 📄

teaching.title: Pass by Value vs Pass by Pointer
teaching.analogy: C ONLY passes by value — it always copies. To 'pass by reference' in C, you pass a POINTER (the address). The function then follows the pointer to modify the original. Like mailing a photocopy vs mailing a map to the original.
teaching.content: C passes by value always. To modify caller's variables: pass their ADDRESS (&) and receive a pointer (*). double swap(int *a, int *b) swaps via pointers. This is how scanf modifies your variable: scanf('%d', &x).

examples.title: Pass by Value vs Pointer
Classic: Swap with Pointers
examples.explanation: The fundamental C pointer interview question.

teaching.funFact: scanf's & requirement (scanf('%d', &n)) is C's pass-by-pointer in action! scanf needs to WRITE to your variable, so it needs its address. Forgetting & is one of the most common C beginner bugs! 🐛

mcq.q: What is a function prototype in C?
options: A declaration telling the compiler the function's signature before its definition
mcq.explanation: Prototype: A function prototype (int add(int,int);) tells the compiler the name, return type, and parameter types BEFORE the full definition. Required when definition comes after the call.

mcq.q: void function(int x){x=99;} after calling with int n=5, n=?
mcq.explanation: Unchanged: C passes by VALUE. x is a copy of n. Modifying x doesn't affect n. n stays 5.

mcq.q: To modify caller's int variable from a function, pass:
mcq.explanation: Pointer: void fn(int *x) receives a pointer. Call with fn(&n). Inside, *x modifies the caller's variable n.

mcq.q: Why does scanf require & before variable?
options: scanf needs the address to WRITE to the variable — it modifies it via pointer
mcq.explanation: scanf Pointer: scanf needs to modify your variable, so it needs its address. scanf('%d', &n) — &n passes the address of n. scanf then stores the input at that address.

mcq.q: C has which function-related feature that Java does NOT?
mcq.explanation: Function Pointers: C can store the address of a function in a pointer: int (*fp)(int,int) = add; fp(5,3); This enables callbacks, dynamic dispatch without OOP, and plugin systems.

coding.title: Prototype syntax
coding.objective: Write the PROTOTYPE (not definition) for: int multiply(int a, int b)
coding.starterCode:
// Prototype — just the declaration:
___ multiply(int a, int b)___
coding.hint: Prototype: returnType name(params); — ends with semicolon.

coding.title: Pass by pointer
coding.objective: To pass int n by pointer: function(___n) — what fills blank?
coding.hint: & gets the address of n. fn(&n) passes the address.

coding.title: Swap trace
coding.explanation: void swap(int*a,int*b){int t=*a;*a=*b;*b=t;} x=3,y=7 after swap?
coding.hint: swap exchanges the values via pointers. x=7, y=3.

options: multiply defined AFTER main without prototype — compiler doesn't know its signature. Add prototype: int multiply(int,int); before main.
debugging.answer: multiply defined AFTER main without prototype — compiler doesn't know its signature. Add prototype: int multiply(int,int); before main.
debugging.hint: C compiler reads top-to-bottom. Define functions before use OR provide a prototype declaration.

teaching.title: Recursion in C
teaching.analogy: C's stack-based execution makes recursion very visual — every function call creates a new stack frame with its own local variables. Recursion stacks these frames, the base case unwinds them.
teaching.content: Recursion in C: same rules as Java. Base case required. Each recursive call gets its OWN stack frame with its own local variables. Stack overflow if too deep. C is famous for recursive algorithms: quicksort, mergesort, tree traversal.

examples.title: Factorial & Fibonacci in C
Stack Visualization
examples.explanation: Each call creates its own frame.

teaching.funFact: C's standard qsort() in <stdlib.h> uses a recursive quicksort internally. Quicksort was invented by Tony Hoare in 1959 — and the C standard library has used it ever since. Elegance that lasts 65 years! ⚡

teaching.title: Storage Classes & Scope
teaching.analogy: C variable scope is controlled by where you declare and three keywords: auto (default local), static (persistent between calls), extern (lives in another file). Static local variables remember their value between function calls — like a variable with amnesia that keeps ONE memory.
teaching.content: Local variables (auto): live on stack, die when function returns. static local: lives for program lifetime, retains value between calls (only initialized once). global: file scope. extern: declared elsewhere. register: hint to use CPU register.

examples.title: static Local Variable

teaching.funFact: static in C and static in Java are COMPLETELY DIFFERENT! C: static local persists between calls. Java: static belongs to the class. They share the word 'static' but mean different things! 🔄

mcq.q: factorial(4) in C with base n<=1 return 1:
static local variable in C — when is it initialized?
options: Once, the first time the function runs
mcq.explanation: Once: static int x=0 inside a function initializes x to 0 only once. Subsequent calls retain and modify x. It lives for the whole program lifetime.

mcq.q: Each recursive call in C creates:
options: A new stack frame with its own local variables
mcq.explanation: New Frame: Each call pushes a new stack frame containing its own local variables and return address. This is why deep recursion causes stack overflow.

mcq.q: Without a base case in a C recursive function:
mcq.explanation: Stack Overflow: Each call pushes a frame. Without base case, the stack fills up → segmentation fault (stack overflow). No compiler error — it's a runtime crash.

coding.title: Factorial base case
coding.objective: int fact(int n){if(n<=___) return 1; return n*fact(n-1);}

coding.title: Static local
coding.objective: static int c=0; c++; — 3 calls. Third call prints?
coding.hint: static retains value: call1→1, call2→2, call3→3.

options: Missing base case — infinite recursion → stack overflow / segfault. Add: if(n<=0) return 0;
debugging.answer: Missing base case — infinite recursion → stack overflow / segfault. Add: if(n<=0) return 0;
debugging.hint: Every recursive C function MUST have a base case!

sublevels.title: FUNCTION POINTERS
sublevels.subtitle: Storing and calling functions via pointers

teaching.title: Function Pointers in C
teaching.analogy: A function pointer is an address in your program's code segment. Instead of calling a function by name, you call it through a pointer — enabling dynamic dispatch, callbacks, and plugin-like systems without OOP.
teaching.content: Function pointer syntax: returnType (*ptrName)(paramTypes). Assign: fp = functionName. Call: fp(args) or (*fp)(args). Used for callbacks (qsort comparators), jump tables, and C's approach to polymorphism.

examples.title: Function Pointers
qsort Callback — Real Use
examples.explanation: C standard library uses function pointers.

teaching.funFact: C's qsort uses a function pointer for comparison, making it work with ANY data type. This is C's version of Java's Comparator interface — the same concept, implemented with raw pointers instead of OOP! 🔀

mcq.q: int (*fp)(int,int) = add; fp(2,3) — what does this call?
mcq.explanation: Calls add: fp points to add. fp(2,3) is equivalent to add(2,3) = 5. Function pointers enable dynamic dispatch.

mcq.q: Which C standard library function uses a function pointer callback?
mcq.explanation: qsort: Takes a comparison function pointer — qsort(arr, n, size, compare). This is C's way of writing generic algorithms that work on any type.

mcq.q: Function pointers in C enable what OOP concept approximately?
mcq.explanation: Dynamic Dispatch: Function pointers let you call different functions through the same pointer — similar to virtual method dispatch in OOP. C structs with function pointers approximate OOP classes!

coding.title: Function pointer syntax
coding.objective: Declare pointer to function int add(int,int). Fill the blank.
coding.starterCode:
int add(int a,int b){return a+b;}
int (___fp)(int,int) = add;
coding.hint: int (*fp)(int,int) — the * goes inside the parentheses with the name.

coding.title: Call via pointer
coding.hint: fp points to add. fp(5,3) = add(5,3) = 8.

options: Type mismatch — fp is pointer to function taking ONE int, but add takes TWO. Declare: int (*fp)(int,int) = add;
debugging.answer: Type mismatch — fp is pointer to function taking ONE int, but add takes TWO. Declare: int (*fp)(int,int) = add;
debugging.hint: Function pointer type must EXACTLY match the function signature — return type AND all parameter types.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE C RUNTIME ENGINE
teaching.analogy: The C Runtime Engine manages every function call, every stack frame, every pointer dereference. It knows C has no objects — only functions, pointers, and raw memory. Defeat it by mastering C's function model completely.
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 5 C: prototypes, pass-by-value vs pass-by-pointer, scanf & requirement, function pointers, recursion + base case, stack frames, static local variables, scope.

examples.title: Full C Function Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ returnType name(params); — prototype
✅ C passes by VALUE always
✅ int *x + fn(&n) for 'by reference'
✅ scanf('%d', &n) — pointer required
✅ static local: persists between calls
✅ Recursion: base case or segfault!
✅ int (*fp)(int,int) — function pointer
✅ qsort() uses callback function pointer

teaching.funFact: The entire Unix/Linux kernel (millions of lines) is written in C using only functions, pointers, and structs. No classes, no exceptions, no garbage collector. C's simplicity IS its power for systems programming! 🐧

mcq.q: void fn(int x){x=99;} int n=5; fn(n); n=?
mcq.explanation: 5: Passed by value. fn gets a copy. n unchanged.

mcq.q: void fn(int *x){*x=99;} int n=5; fn(&n); n=?
mcq.explanation: 99: Passed by pointer. *x=99 dereferences and sets n to 99.

mcq.q: Static local int c=0; called 4 times with c++. 4th call prints?
mcq.explanation: 4: static retains value. Calls: 1,2,3,4. Fourth call: c=4.

mcq.q: C's equivalent of Java's pass-by-reference is:
options: Passing a pointer to the variable
mcq.explanation: Pointer: C achieves 'pass by reference' by passing a pointer: fn(&n). The function dereferences (*n) to access/modify the original.

options: A pointer to a function taking two ints and returning int
mcq.explanation: Function Pointer: int (*fp)(int,int) = pointer fp to a function that takes two ints and returns int. Assign with fp=functionName.

coding.title: Boss: Pointer swap
coding.objective: void swap(int*a,int*b). Call to swap x=5,y=8?

options: Missing & — scanf needs the ADDRESS to write to. Use: scanf('%d', &n);
debugging.answer: Missing & — scanf needs the ADDRESS to write to. Use: scanf('%d', &n);
debugging.hint: scanf modifies n — needs its address. &n gives the address. Without &, n's VALUE is passed as an address → undefined behavior!
